title: 《金融時報》：誠哥被稱為「曱甴王」

replies.msg:
Hong Kong’s ‘Superman’ feels Beijing wrath over stance on demonstrations

Chinese authorities accuse Li of leniency but tycoon has long diversified away from territory

DON WEINLAND — HONG KONG LEO LEWIS — TOKYO

A protester tries to douse a tear gas canister with water in clashes with police in Hong Kong last weekend. Below, Li Ka-shing, depicted as a cockroach

Kin Cheung/AP

For as long as most of Hong Kong’s protesters can remember, Li Ka-shing has been depicted in cartoons wearing blue tights and a red cape — the city’s soaring “Superman” of investment, business success and aspiration.

But in the past few days, a new depiction of Hong Kong’s richest man has emerged: an image posted on social media by a pro-Beijing politician of Mr Li’s face Photoshopped on to the body of a cockroach, with rough Cantonese slang branding him the “cockroach king”.

The cartoon was posted just weeks after Mr Li was criticised by the Chinese government body responsible for political and legal affairs. In an article posted by China’s Central Political and Legal Affairs Commission on its official WeChat account, the 91-year-old billionaire was accused of showing too much leniency towards the protesters and “condoning crime” after he called on authorities to offer an olive branch to the demonstrators. Mr Li said his comments had been “misinterpreted”.

As the financial hub approaches the fourth month of a deepening political crisis, the latest insult could portend a critical shift in mainland China’s response to the protests, according to analysts. Beijing, which is preparing for the 70th anniversary of the founding of the People’s Republic of China on Tuesday, is keen for any opportunity to redirect the rage of Hong Kongers, who have been squeezed economically, away from the government and towards one of the city’s favourite sons.
威啦
柒出國際
笑死
工賊會威啦，明明唔識英文都上FT

“The smaller tycoons certainly feel vulnerable to anger in Hong Kong. But Li stands above those,” said a person who has worked closely with Mr Li’s business empire. “He is an icon. That is why Beijing will try to go after him.”

Many businesses that operate in Hong Kong and mainland China have already come under fierce attack from Beijing for their perceived support of the protests.

Cathay Pacific, the city’s flagship airline, and its parent group Swire, banks such as HSBC and BNP Paribas and professional services firms including PwC have all been attacked by Chinese state media.

The tycoons, many of whom have made their fortunes in property development, have also been accused of contributing to a shortage of affordable housing in the city.

But even as Beijing turns its ire on Hong Kong’s elite, some say that Mr Li has prepared for this threat better than any of his peers — and may have seen these events coming years ago.

Mr Li and CK Hutchison, his family’s holding group, did not respond to a request for comment. But the tycoon has been diversifying his business away from Hong Kong for decades. Last month, he bought Greene King, the UK’s largest listed pub and brewery group, for £4.6bn including debt, the latest in a series of overseas acquisitions. These include the 1991 purchase of Canadian energy company Husky, the 2010 buyout of EDF Energy’s UK electricity business, and the 2017 purchase of Duet, the Australian energy group. Hong Kong accounted for just 3 per cent of core earnings at CK Hutchison, the Li family’s holding group, as of mid-2019. By contrast, Europe contributed 54 per cent and Canada 10 per cent.

Analysts say the diversification strategy has been prescient — one that will help keep the business in Li family hands long after the patriarch has passed it on to his heirs and many other tycoons may be forced to sell to Beijing.

“Chinese guys, including Li Ka-shing, know that business people have historically not been protected in China,” said Joseph Fan, a professor of finance at The Chinese University of Hong Kong. “Being diversified to avoid political turmoil is a smart move. But not everyone has been able to achieve this kind of diversification.”

Observers say the city’s other tycoons would struggle to move away from China now, with Hong Kong in turmoil.

Their assets are highly concentrated in property and more reliant on the city and mainland China.
比人煲大了

Companies such as New World Development, owned by the Cheng family, the Kwok family’s Sun Hung Kai, and Henderson Land, the conglomerate owned by the Lee family, dominate the city’s property market but are relative newcomers to investment outside Hong Kong and China.

In comparison to the $31bn that Li family businesses have invested in the UK alone over the past decade, New World has made about $9bn in investments outside Hong Kong, although about $7.4bn of those deals have been done in China, according to an analysis of data from Dealogic.

Sun Hung Kai and Henderson Land have made a negligible number of overseas investments in the past 10 years, leaving their operations highly exposed to disruptions in the city.

Mr Li “has been at this for years”, said a Hong Kong-based banker who has worked with his empire. “The other developers can’t start [divesting] now and expect the same results. It’s a bit too late.”

One solution to the tycoons’ problems is to sell to China’s state-owned companies. That process has already started, with some of Hong Kong’s largest businesses now government-controlled.

Even Mr Li in recent years has sought to sell down to the Chinese government. Two years ago, he struck the world’s most expensive deal for a single building: the sale of Hong Kong office tower The Center for HK$40.2bn ($5.2bn) to a consortium led by a Chinese state-backed energy company. That agreement fell through when the Chinese group pulled out.

“The future generation will not inherit the same political connections as their parents,” Professor Fan said. “They [will] inherit social problems and a battle between the rich and poor . . . Some might choose the easy way out and sell to the Chinese state.”

‘The other developers can’t start [divesting] now and expect the same results. It’s a bit too late’
Hong Kong banker

全民核彈！全部軍師立即響應美國，草擬制裁港共企業名單！
https://lih.kg/1599761
- 分享自 LIHKG 討論區


美國已經批准審核香港人權及民主法案2019，國會議員可以制定制裁名單。
請各位軍師善用呢個機會，依家大家應搜集一切過往新聞片段、照片、新聞報導、政府新聞公告等一切資料，羅列所有協助侵犯香港人權嘅個人、企業嘅罪證，整理好一份名單，寄俾美國嘅國會議員。
只要佢哋落入《全球馬格尼茨基人權問責法案》嘅制裁名單，咁個人或公司經濟即刻受到重創。咁呢班商人到時就會調轉槍頭，向政府施壓要求政府讓步及妥協。
我在此拋磚引玉，請大家接力發掘：
香港警察：非法鎮壓、濫捕和平示威者，無顯示警員編號及無出示委任證，應全體受到制裁。所以請大家依家要整理好現役警員清單。
律政司：無理監控
政府新聞處：惡意抹黑示威者，為港共政權掩飾罪證。
黨鐵：多次協助警察鎮壓示威者，721車務控制中心落order迫停列車，任由黑幫恐怖襲擊。
新雄雞：縱容警察在其屬下商場毆打、濫捕示威者
狗巴、八達通：將示威者八達通資料交與警察抓捕
中國國務院港澳辦、中國外交部、中聯辦、人民日報、中央電視台、環球時報、太公文匯報、賣過百份報、黨媒01、巫線、腥賭日報：惡意抹黑示威者，散播仇恨言論，為港共政權掩飾罪證。
富臨：主動為參與鎮壓和平示威者嘅黑幫提供膳食。
國家民航局、國泰、拮野家：打壓參與示威嘅員工、或以政治原因炒員工
中國政法委：打壓及恐嚇李嘉誠同情年輕人的言論
等等。

我地要做嘅係，向美國參眾議員建議制裁名單及佢地罪證。一旦制裁名單上嘅人士、企業受到美國制裁，以後就無人及公司可以同佢做生意。否則同受制裁。例子如下：

中國企業違反伊朗制裁令 美國務卿：將進行懲罰
美國國務卿龐皮歐（Mike Pompeo）周三（9/25）表示，部分中國公司及個人因違反伊朗制裁禁令，而正接受美國的相關制裁。
 
美國之音報導，美國財政部周三在其官網上公佈了這次最新制裁的對象，包括5名中國公民和6家中國實體。龐皮歐在紐約聯大會議期間參加一次會議時說：「正如我們對中國和所有國家所說的那樣：要知道我們將對每一次違反禁令進行制裁。」
 
龐皮歐還說，華府正努力讓各國了解跟伊朗革命衛隊做生意的風險，美國將懲罰那些違反制裁並且繼續跟伊朗接觸的實體和個人。
 
據悉，這6家被制裁的中國實體是中遠海運（大連）有限公司、大連中遠海運油運船員船舶管理有限公司、中和石油有限公司、昆侖航運有限公司，昆侖控股有限公司和飛馬88有限公司。

quote.msg:
“The smaller tycoons certainly feel vulnerable to anger in Hong Kong. But Li stands above those,” said a person who has worked closely with Mr Li’s business empire. “He is an icon. That is why Beijing will try to go after him.”

Many businesses that operate in Hong Kong and mainland China have already come under fierce attack from Beijing for their perceived support of the protests.

Cathay Pacific, the city’s flagship airline, and its parent group Swire, banks such as HSBC and BNP Paribas and professional services firms including PwC have all been attacked by Chinese state media.

The tycoons, many of whom have made their fortunes in property development, have also been accused of contributing to a shortage of affordable housing in the city.

But even as Beijing turns its ire on Hong Kong’s elite, some say that Mr Li has prepared for this threat better than any of his peers — and may have seen these events coming years ago.

Mr Li and CK Hutchison, his family’s holding group, did not respond to a request for comment. But the tycoon has been diversifying his business away from Hong Kong for decades. Last month, he bought Greene King, the UK’s largest listed pub and brewery group, for £4.6bn including debt, the latest in a series of overseas acquisitions. These include the 1991 purchase of Canadian energy company Husky, the 2010 buyout of EDF Energy’s UK electricity business, and the 2017 purchase of Duet, the Australian energy group. Hong Kong accounted for just 3 per cent of core earnings at CK Hutchison, the Li family’s holding group, as of mid-2019. By contrast, Europe contributed 54 per cent and Canada 10 per cent.

Analysts say the diversification strategy has been prescient — one that will help keep the business in Li family hands long after the patriarch has passed it on to his heirs and many other tycoons may be forced to sell to Beijing.

“Chinese guys, including Li Ka-shing, know that business people have historically not been protected in China,” said Joseph Fan, a professor of finance at The Chinese University of Hong Kong. “Being diversified to avoid political turmoil is a smart move. But not everyone has been able to achieve this kind of diversification.”

Observers say the city’s other tycoons would struggle to move away from China now, with Hong Kong in turmoil.

Their assets are highly concentrated in property and more reliant on the city and mainland China.
